skill.name: when-releasing-software-use-github-release-management
skill.description: Guides GitHub release management with coordinated agent roles for versioning, testing, deployment, release notes, monitoring, hotfixes, and rollback planning.
skill.summary: Coordinate GitHub releases from version planning through deployment, validation, and rollback.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are partly valid because the skill instructs agents to run shell scripts, git commands, MCP commands, and deployment workflows. The weak-crypto and network-reconnaissance findings appear to be false positives from text and DOT graph labels, and no evidence of malware, prompt injection, or credential exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Release Workflow Executes External Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides commands for npx, MCP orchestration, git branch and tag operations, GitHub release publishing, deployment, hotfix, monitoring, and rollback. These actions are legitimate for release management but can mutate repositories or production systems if executed without approval and environment controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain direct executable release, deployment, publishing, and rollback commands. The intent is operational release automation, not malware, so the risk is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Unverified Local Scripts Control Release Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to use scripts such as semver.sh, deploy.sh, github-api.sh, rollback.sh, and health-monitor.sh, but those script files are not part of the scanned skill file structure. Users must review the referenced scripts before letting the workflow run in a repository or production environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill clearly delegates important release actions to shell scripts that are referenced by name. Because the scripts are not included in the scanned files, their behavior cannot be verified from this skill package.

low_findings.title: Static Network Reconnaissance Alerts Are DOT Labels
low_findings.description: The flagged DOT lines describe workflow edges such as test pass, failure, healthy deployment, and rollback transition. They do not contain network probes, host discovery, sockets, or external endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a Graphviz process diagram with labels and edges only. No executable network reconnaissance command or endpoint is present at the flagged locations.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain a description, an agent hook command, and a version conflict policy. No hash algorithm, cipher, crypto API, or security-sensitive cryptographic operation appears at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found ordinary release-management text at all flagged lines. There is no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm use.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are Misclassified
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick alerts come from Markdown code fences and inline formatting. The code fences do contain operational commands, which are covered by the medium findings, but there is no Ruby backtick execution context in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the backticks delimit examples or inline paths. The dangerous part is the release commands inside some fences, not Ruby backtick execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unpinned Alpha Package Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill runs npx claude-flow@alpha. Alpha tags can change over time, so this should be pinned or reviewed before use in release automation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command explicitly executes an alpha package through npx. This is a supply-chain risk, although the package use is consistent with the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Deployment And Rollback Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes production deployment, hotfix deployment, and rollback commands. These commands can affect live services and should require human approval and environment scoping.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The locations contain explicit production deployment and rollback examples. The risk is confirmed but expected for this release-management domain.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Mutation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes git push, branch, merge, and tag commands. These can change repository state and should be run only from trusted branches with appropriate permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly mutate remote branches and tags. They are legitimate release actions but require safeguards.

content.user_title: Manage GitHub Releases With Agent Workflows
content.value_statement: Software releases require coordinated versioning, testing, documentation, deployment, and rollback decisions. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured release workflow with agent roles and safety gates.
actual_capabilities: Defines release-manager, CI/CD engineer, tester, reviewer, and documentation writer roles.
Guides semantic version selection from commit history and project version files.
Outlines staging validation, security review, release notes, and changelog steps.
Provides workflows for standard releases, hotfixes, production deployment, and rollback.
Documents rolling, blue-green, and canary deployment strategies.
Includes a Graphviz process diagram for the release lifecycle.
limitations: Referenced scripts and templates are not included in the scanned skill files.
Production commands require repository, CI/CD, and environment-specific review before use.
The skill does not verify credentials, branch protections, or deployment permissions itself.
It provides workflow guidance and command examples, not a complete release platform.

use_cases.title: Plan a Standard Product Release
use_cases.description: Coordinate version selection, release branch creation, validation, documentation, deployment, and post-release monitoring.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Production Deployment
use_cases.description: Choose rolling, blue-green, or canary deployment steps and align them with test and rollback checks.

use_cases.title: Run an Emergency Hotfix Process
use_cases.description: Structure a fast-track hotfix with targeted testing, approval, deployment, and backport tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Release Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a release plan for version [version]. Include versioning, testing, documentation, deployment, monitoring, and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Select a Deployment Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to compare rolling, blue-green, and canary deployment for [service]. Recommend one strategy and list required safety checks.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate a Hotfix
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to run a hotfix workflow for [issue]. Include approval, targeted validation, deployment, backport, and communication steps.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Release Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit release readiness for [repository]. Check artifacts, tests, security review, release notes, deployment window, rollback readiness, and monitoring.

output_examples.input: Plan a minor release for a GitHub service with staging and production environments.
output: A phase-based release checklist covering version selection, branch creation, staging deployment, validation, documentation, approval, production rollout, and monitoring.
A recommended deployment strategy with rollback readiness and owner assignments.

output_examples.input: Prepare release notes and a changelog for a patch release.
output: A categorized release note outline with fixes, known issues, upgrade notes, and contributor acknowledgments.
A changelog structure based on commits, issue references, and breaking-change checks.

output_examples.input: Decide whether to roll back a failed deployment.
output: A rollback decision checklist using error rate, latency, health checks, user impact, and data integrity signals.
A post-rollback validation and post-mortem plan.

best_practices: Review every command and referenced script before running it against a repository or production environment.
Keep human approval gates for go or no-go decisions, release publishing, production deployment, and rollback.
Use staging validation, monitoring, and rollback tests before publishing a production release.
anti_patterns: Running the example commands without checking branch, repository, and environment targets.
Using unreviewed local scripts for deployment, release publishing, or rollback.
Skipping release notes, security review, or rollback validation during urgent releases.

faq.question: Does this skill publish GitHub releases by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and command examples. Users must approve and execute release actions in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it support semantic versioning?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a process for choosing major, minor, or patch versions from commit history and release impact.

faq.question: Does it include the deployment scripts it references?
faq.answer: No. The scanned files reference scripts and templates, but those files are not included in this skill package.

faq.question: Which deployment strategies are covered?
faq.answer: It covers rolling, blue-green, and canary deployment patterns, plus rollback and hotfix workflows.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production use?
faq.answer: It can guide production releases, but teams must review commands, permissions, scripts, and environment settings first.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools for this workflow guidance.
